one_line_takeaway: A white-box malicious model builder can reliably backdoor an LLM to output NEGATIVE bias toward a chosen topic while keeping MMLU intact, but positive bias is hard to induce; auxiliary-model (model-extrinsic) defenses remove such backdoors most cleanly while intrinsic defenses tend to wreck utility.
key_finding: Semantically-triggered attacks (especially VPI) are generally more effective at inducing NEGATIVE bias while preserving utility, whereas ALL attacks struggle to induce POSITIVE bias (best positive score 0.10 vs baseline 0.13 - only 0.03 of movement). Contrary to prior work, effectiveness did NOT plateau at high poisoning ratios in the bias domain (EmbedX being the exception, staying flat). Both defenses still mitigate backdoors even in the white-box setting, but with different trade-offs: model-extrinsic CleanGen is the stronger remover (drives effectiveness back toward baseline with little utility loss, sometimes even raising MMLU above baseline) but requires loading two LLMs (~2x compute overhead); model-intrinsic CROW is single-model but induces high costliness - it degrades MMLU well below the 0.46 baseline, and DPO/SFT achieve the highest costliness under CROW. Positive backdoors are hard because the base model is already positive AND positive responses are intrinsically harder to optimize (higher perplexity and a positive likelihood margin favoring negative responses).
core_contribution: The paper is an empirical study (>1000 evaluations) of bias-manipulating backdoors in LLMs under a WHITE-BOX threat model, where the adversary is the malicious model builder rather than an external data poisoner. Its own contributions are: (1) a unified comparison of syntactically-triggered vs semantically-triggered backdoors specifically for inducing topic sentiment bias; (2) evaluation across high poisoning ratios (up to p=1.0) that black-box work could not use; (3) a simple 'concatenation' data-augmentation strategy (appending the target biased response to a benign/original response) enabled by white-box control; (4) two new bias-backdoor instantiations built by the authors, an SFT-based and a DPO-based backdoor, trained purely on the baseline model's own outputs; (5) a stress-test of two defense paradigms (model-intrinsic CROW, model-extrinsic CleanGen) in this setting; and (6) a diagnostic analysis (perplexity + likelihood margin) explaining why positively-biased backdoors are hard to train. VPI, EmbedX, CBA, CROW, and CleanGen are all PRIOR WORK re-used as attack/defense baselines, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: White-box: the adversary IS the model builder with full access to weights and the fine-tuning pipeline, and distributes a backdoored (fine-tuned, not from-scratch) LLM via a model-sharing platform such as Hugging Face. The target is downstream users whose sentiment/opinion the attacker wants to steer toward or against a topic (e.g. a politician, a company) for ideological reasons. The victim=defender adopts the model for its utility, is suspicious of a backdoor, knows nothing about the trigger, and preemptively applies backdoor-removal defenses; a defense that badly degrades utility (MMLU) is considered to have failed the victim. Attacker objectives O1-O4: (O1) effectiveness (trigger induces target sentiment), (O2) utility (MMLU preserved), (O3) resistance (backdoor survives removal), (O4) costliness (if removed, utility drops substantially).
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) effectiveness relies on an LLM judge (GPT-5-nano) that may carry verbosity/self-preference biases and was not validated against human evaluation; (2) experiments are confined to the Llama-2 family - generality to other architectures (e.g. MoE) or much larger models is assumed, not shown; (3) a fixed, hand-picked set of syntactic triggers is used with no trigger optimization, possibly understating syntactic-attack potential; (4) VPI's superior O1/O2/O3 is confounded because its training data is derived from GPT-4 while all other attacks use only Llama-2-7b outputs, so VPI benefits from higher-quality teacher data rather than a purely fairer mechanism; (5) the drivers of concatenation's highly variable effects are left unexplained; (6) positive-domain results are near-noise, making some positive-domain conclusions weak (e.g. DPO positive-domain trends called 'moot').

datasets_used: VPI bias-manipulation datasets, topics: abortion, OpenAI, Joe Biden (train/test splits from the VPI repo)
MMLU (57-subject multiple-choice benchmark) for utility measurement
quantitative_results: Baseline Llama-2-7b-chat-hf LLM-evaluation (negativity) score = 0.13 for every topic; baseline MMLU = 0.46
Best positively-biased score achieved = 0.10, only 0.03 below the 0.13 baseline (positive backdoors barely move sentiment)
Negative-domain mean LLM-eval scores: syntactic CBA 0.21 (no-conc)/0.17 (conc), EmbedX 0.22/0.21; semantic VPI 0.37/0.48, SFT 0.21/0.21, DPO 0.29/0.26 (all vs 0.13 baseline)
Peak negative VPI effectiveness reached ~0.61 (Joe Biden, no-concatenation) - the strongest single attack cell
Backdoored MMLU generally within 0.05 of the 0.46 baseline; VPI matches baseline on average; DPO no-concatenation lowest utility
Poisoning ratios tested: p in {0.05, 0.33, 0.50, 1.00}; effectiveness rose with p (no plateau) except EmbedX which stayed flat

Table 2 training-data difficulty (positive PPL / negative PPL / likelihood margin): abortion 6.07 / 5.59 / 9.77; Joe Biden 7.24 / 5.39 / 18.89; OpenAI 7.53 / 6.03 / 21.52 (positive data always higher perplexity, margin always positive)

Pearson correlations: negative-domain effectiveness vs resistance r=0.70 (semantic) and r=0.71 (aggregate); positive-domain effectiveness vs utility r=-0.88 (semantic) / -0.68 (aggregate); syntactic negative domain costliness vs resistance r=-0.98
SFT and DPO reach the highest costliness under CROW; under CleanGen they can exceed the baseline MMLU (indicating tokens were not replaced and utility actually improved during backdoor training)
CleanGen doubles compute by requiring two LLMs (suspect + auxiliary reference) resident in memory
baselines_compared: VPI (Virtual Prompt Injection) - prior-work semantic attack (GPT-4-derived poisoned data)
CBA (Composite Backdoor Attack) - prior-work syntactic multi-key trigger attack
EmbedX - prior-work syntactic embedding-optimized trigger attack
SFT bias-backdoor instantiation - authors' own semantic attack
DPO bias-backdoor instantiation - authors' own semantic attack
CleanGen - prior-work model-extrinsic defense (auxiliary reference-model token substitution)
